pattern: The Sieve of Eratosthenes marks composite numbers by iterating multiples of each prime up to sqrt(n), leaving only the primes unmarked; the count is the number of unmarked entries in [2, n-1].
hints: Testing every candidate individually for primality is far too slow for n up to 5,000,000 — think about eliminating many composites in one sweep.
Start with a boolean array of size n, all set to True (prime candidate). For each number p starting from 2, if it is still marked prime, mark all multiples of p starting from p*p as composite.
You only need to sieve up to sqrt(n); after that, everything still marked True in the range [2, n-1] is prime. Sum the marks to get the count.

edge_inputs.description: n=0 — no numbers to consider, result is 0
n=1 — still no primes strictly less than 1
n=2 — no prime is strictly less than 2
n=3 — only prime strictly less than 3 is 2 → 1
n=4 — only prime strictly less than 4 is 2 and 3 → 2
n exactly on a prime — prime n itself is not counted (strictly less than n)

stress.description: random n across a wide range
stress.note: covers small and large values including near the constraint ceiling
